medicinal.use: Traditionally used in Guyana: crushed leaves juice mixed with breast milk given by teaspoon to children for thrush
Traditionally used for blood cleansing through improved kidney function; bitter leaf and stem tea
Traditionally used as chew-sticks for teeth cleansing and gum strengthening; crushed leaves as paste on forehead for headaches
Traditionally used to strengthen the heart and as a laxative; fresh crushed fruit or used as hot condiment
Traditionally used for blood cleansing through diuretic activity; leaf tea
Traditionally used for high blood pressure (locally called 'pressure'); whole plant boiled as tea
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea

culinary: Ripe fruits eaten, especially by children

medicinal.use: Traditionally used for diarrhea; stem infusion of liana in hot water
Traditionally used for ringworm and other skin fungal conditions; crushed leaf and stem extract applied topically
Traditionally used for fevers; bitter tea from above-ground organs. Also used ritually to repel negative spiritual forces from homes
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea
Traditionally used as a tonic and aphrodisiac for older men (fermented brew with multiple roots); roots also used for diarrhea and gonorrhea
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea
Traditionally used for kidney problems and blood cleansing; red-veined leaf tea
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea
Traditionally used for pain relief applied as crushed leaf poultice (especially headaches); also used as a douche for postpartum cleansing
Traditionally used for female hemorrhaging; whole plant brewed with red buck banana roots and rice bran, strained and ingested
Traditionally used for skin rashes, eczema, and fungal diseases (ringworm, athlete's foot); leaves cooked with salt and applied warm as poultice
Traditionally used for digestive flatulence ('for wind'); several cloves boiled to concentrated mixture and ingested
Traditionally used for dysentery, intestinal worms, and diarrhea; peeled dry bark and leaves boiled as tea
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea
Traditionally used for shortness of breath (heart problems) and indigestion as root tea; syrup from roots used for chest colds
Traditionally used for colds, fevers, malaria, and postpartum cleansing; plant boiled with other herbs in steam bath (patient sits in covered tub to perspire)
Traditionally used for male impotence; boiled with sand bitters, coraila, uru-balli or mixed with barks
Traditionally used to strengthen backs (male sexual stamina), treat female hemorrhaging, and promote healing of cuts and ulcers; leaf infusion and crushed leaves
Traditionally used for postpartum problems (leaf tea), upset stomach, and colds (root and dried flower tea)

culinary: Dried parched seeds used as coffee substitute

medicinal.use: Traditionally used for blood cleansing; tuber brew
Traditionally used externally for eczema and skin problems; plant boiled with salt and liquid applied topically
Traditionally used for nematodes and flatworms; leaf and twig tea sometimes combined with Passiflora foetida
Traditionally used for skin problems; boiled leaves used in baths
Traditionally used for weak but rapid heart rate; leaf tea, especially combined with soursop leaves. Also used as a 'pap' porridge mixture for thrush in children
Traditionally used for blood cleansing, diabetes, and high blood pressure; leaf tea
Traditionally used for excessive female mucous discharge; leaves boiled with salt
Traditionally used for skin ulcers; paste of crushed leaves mixed with salt, vegetable oil and bread applied topically
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea, often combined with other plants

Traditionally used for postpartum cleansing and pain; plant also used in complex formulas for dysmenorrhea and female infertility (combined with minnie root, gully root, broad-leaved thyme, dry granadilla and black sage)
Traditionally used for asthma and bronchitis (strong leaf tea); cataracts (leaf juice squeezed into eyes)
Traditionally used for intestinal nematodes and flatworms; whole plant boiled as tea
Traditionally used for heart palpitations; boiled leaf extracts, especially combined with pastalon (Ludwigia erecta)
Traditionally used for blood cleansing, skin cleansing, and biliousness; bitter leaf tea
Traditionally used for blood cleansing and influenza; bitter tea from leaves and buds
Traditionally used as blood cleanser and purgative; leaf beverage. Distributed across Mexico, Central America, and tropical South America. External uses documented in Colombia and Peru
Traditionally used for ulcers and infections including bladder and vaginal infections; leaf and stem tea
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea
Traditionally used for heat rash; whole plant boiled and beverage applied topically or consumed
Traditionally used for blood purification and dysentery; whole plant boiled as tea
Traditionally used for colds, urinary tract discomfort, and morning sickness; whole plant boiled as sour tea
Traditionally used to stimulate digestion and strengthen the heart; fresh fruit or condiment consumed. Leaves steeped in hot water for blood cleansing
Traditionally used for blood cleansing; leaf tea
Traditionally used for colds and coughs; leaves combined with zeb grass or sorrel, sometimes with lime, boiled to thick liquid infused with honey or sugar
Traditionally used for blood cleansing and influenza; purple leaf tea

contraindications: Extremely toxic — slight overdose could be fatal
Appropriate dosage very difficult to predict
Toxic plant — for external use only in this context
Pregnancy — emmenagogue properties noted in related species
Contains powerful alkaloids (vincristine, vinblastine) — not for unsupervised internal use
Toxic berries — do not consume fruit
Unripe green berries are highly toxic
Ambiguous species identity — multiple Smilax spp. may be involved
